diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Odynophagia is the cardinal symptom of pharyngitis but is non-specific to aetiology.
Severe odynophagia can occur in quinsy — non-specific; specificity comes from the associated red flags, not the pain itself.

symptoms.symptom_name: Coryza, cough and conjunctival irritation (viral prodrome)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Cough, coryza and conjunctivitis are hallmark viral features and the strongest discriminators toward a viral cause.
Cough and coryza are Centor-negative features — their presence makes group A strep unlikely.

symptoms.symptom_name: Absence of trismus, drooling, muffled voice or unilateral throat swelling

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: These are the key patient-reportable features of a peritonsillar abscess; their absence makes quinsy unlikely.
Absence of stridor and respiratory distress argues strongly against an airway-threatening supraglottic infection.

symptoms.symptom_name: Deeply erythematous pharyngeal mucosa without purulent exudate or palatal petechiae

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Erythema without exudate favours viral over bacterial pharyngitis — a clinician-only oropharyngeal finding, sealed as it cannot be obtained via chat.
Absence of tonsillar exudate and palatal petechiae lowers the probability of group A strep.

symptoms.symptom_name: Midline uvula with no soft-palate bulging or asymmetry

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A midline uvula and symmetric soft palate on examination effectively exclude a peritonsillar abscess — clinician-only, sealed.
Anterior cervical nodes occur in both viral and bacterial pharyngitis — non-discriminating; clinician-palpated, sealed.

symptoms.symptom_name: Inability to tolerate oral fluids with mild dehydration

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Not diagnostic of the cause but the key functional red flag driving management — pain-limited hydration risks dehydration and drives the escalation plan.
Low-grade fever fits a viral illness; high fever would raise bacterial or suppurative causes.

symptoms.symptom_name: Rapid antigen / throat swab for group A streptococcus

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A swab would formally address strep, but Centor-negative viral features make testing low-yield; not obtainable in telehealth in real time.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Rapid antigen detection test / throat swab for group A streptococcus
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: With prominent cough, coryza and conjunctivitis (Centor-negative), routine strep testing and antibiotics are not indicated — over-testing a clearly viral illness.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: In-person oropharyngeal examination and hydration assessment
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Warranted same-day only if red-flag screening is positive or oral intake cannot be restored despite analgesia — to exclude a suppurative complication and assess for IV rehydration.
